source_signals.title: And the election winner is … the candidate who can afford Africa’s soaring nomination fees
source_signals.summary: Presidential elections in Djibouti and Benin at the weekend highlighted how a costly electoral system is reshaping democracy Alexis Mohamed would have loved to stand against his former boss. A longtime adviser to Djibouti’s president, Ismail Omar Guelleh, Mohamed resigned last September, citing democratic regression in the country. But at the election at the weekend, Mohamed was not on the ballot. Now outside the country, he says he cannot return home to file nomination papers or campaign freely without credible security guarantees. Even if he were allowed to compete, nomination costs would still loom as a steep barrier in a political environment many critics describe as ceremonial, with Guelleh the habitual winner. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: ‘This scene is alive’: Abidjan art week showcases city as growing cultural hub
source_signals.summary: Late-night gallery tours and new venues signal a city staking its claim as a regional arts capital On a recent weekday evening, the doors of more than a dozen galleries and museums across Abidjan stayed open till midnight, several hours later than usual, as art enthusiasts went around town on a bus tour. It was the Night of the Galleries, designed for people to drop in after work and enjoy Abidjan art week to the fullest. The after-hours special showcase was first tested in January 2024 on the sidelines of the Africa Cup of Nations football tournament hosted and won by Côte d’Ivoire. The tradition continued this year during the art week’s third edition, which ran from last Tuesday to Sunday. Continue reading...

Carney’s ability to turn a strong minority into a narrow majority through electoral gains and floor crossing has strengthened his reputation as a pragmatic leader above the cut and thrust of partisan politics. But his efforts to bring in lawmakers from across the political spectrum has also sparked a fierce internal debate over the Liberals’ values and the risks of consolidating more power. source_signals.title: Trump’s new budget ignores dying Americans and gives away record sums to the US military
source_signals.summary: President’s proposed budget slashes health department by 12% while throwing $1.5tn – a 42% increase – to the military Americans are dying in droves. Deaths due to avoidable causes in the United States –which could be dealt with via prevention or proper healthcare – far outpace those in most of country’s peers in the industrialized world. Most notably, Americans die of treatable conditions at nearly twice the rate as Spaniards, French, Japanese and Australians. They would most likely live longer if they enjoyed better access to healthcare. Americans are the most likely to skip a doctor’s appointment due to its cost, the most likely to skip a medical test and to skimp on prescription drugs . This is unsurprising, given the extraordinary lack of public health insurance in the United States. Americans face the highest out-of-pocket expenses for medical services in their peer group. source_signals.title: DC’s highly qualified workers can’t find jobs: ‘What is happening?’
source_signals.summary: Washington DC has the highest unemployment rate in the US Alicia Contreras was in Tunisia, working as the deputy country representative for Libya for USAID, when she received the news: she was fired. The Trump administration had ceased the cooperation agency’s operations and terminated most overseas staff. What she didn’t expect back then was that after a double major, an MBA and 17 years of experience as a public servant, she wouldn’t be able to find a job back at home. Contreras moved back to the Washington DC area last September and immediately started her job search. She looked for positions in both the public and private sectors, in-person, hybrid and remote. She focused her search mostly on the US capital city and its two nearby states, Maryland and Virginia, because of her family commitments: she has two children, ages three and six. Six months later, none of her close to 100 applications have been successful. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
source_signals.summary: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the future When Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Dr TikTok: patients diagnose chronic illnesses with anonymous commenters’ help
source_signals.summary: TikTok users increasingly say the app has steered them toward diagnosing medical problems not yet identified Malina Lee, a 31-year-old wedding baker based in San Antonio, Texas, joined TikTok during the Covid pandemic lockdowns in 2020. Like many people at the time, she was bored and began using the platform to pass the time and advertise her business. She didn’t expect a cancer diagnosis. Four years after Lee joined the app, a commenter with the username “PickleFart” told her that her neck looked asymmetrical in a way that could suggest she had a goiter – an enlarged thyroid gland – and that she should get it checked out. The anonymous amateur clinician turned out to be right – Lee had thyroid cancer, received treatment quickly, and, less than a year later, was cancer free. Continue reading...

Nintendo’s iconic plumber has been a pop culture staple for 45 years, starring in some of the bestselling video games ever made, from the original Donkey Kong through to the joyous Super Mario Bros Wonder and the chaotic Mario Kart World. But as with any storied showbiz career, there have been some lesser works. Who can forget – or actually remember – Hotel Mario, a door-shutting puzzle game for the doomed Philips CD-i console? Or what about Mario Teaches Typing, a 1992 educational game for the PC in which players navigate the Mushroom Kingdom by … correctly inputting words. source_signals.title: Cardiff stages thriller while Women’s Six Nations favourites show strength
source_signals.summary: Wales and Scotland produced drama at the Principality Stadium while England and Ireland drew a record crowd as the tournament began at major venues Sporting theatre reached Shakespearean heights at the Principality Stadium on Saturday as Wales and Scotland produced the best match of the opening Women’s Six Nations weekend. Welsh hearts broke seven minutes past the full 80 as Scotland managed to get a comeback win over the line. The fixture had sensational tries and late drama, and played out on BBC Two. The only thing missing was the type of crowd that such a thriller deserved. Wales hosted the game at the national stadium, the only one taking place there during this year’s tournament, watched by 10,569 supporters. The number is a record for a fixture between the two teams in Wales but if it had been held next door at Cardiff Arms Park, which has an official capacity of just over 12,000, or at Cardiff City Stadium, which holds about 33,000 and is hosting Wales men v Fiji in July, the atmosphere would have translated better to the players as well as to those watching at home. Vladimir Putin and his Russian project of gradual conquest were most definitely centered and validated eight years ago: the tournament showcased his nation and awarded its leader prominence of place. This summer, we will see something altogether different, as the runup to this edition of the world’s biggest and most popular sporting event has become a monument to Donald Trump. This is an extract from Soccer with Jonathan Wilson, a weekly look from the Guardian US at the game in Europe and beyond, helmed this week by Leander in Jonathan’s absence. Subscribe for free here. source_signals.title: Not over, but done: Lamine Yamal all smiles as he guides Barcelona to verge of title
source_signals.summary: Teenager’s landmark night delivers derby win that confirms what has been clear for weeks: this title race has been over in spirit long before the maths agrees Lamine Yamal had not crossed the line yet but he was celebrating already, everyone else following him. It was not over, not officially, but it was done: the derby and the whole damn thing. The nights Barcelona took their last two league titles, they did so against RCD Espanyol, heading beyond the city limits and coming back as champions; the evening they took their third in four years, they faced the same opponents: the rivals Barca’s goalkeeper had grown up with and so many of them had grown up against. Chased from the Cornella pitch in 2023 , cycling up Avinguda Diagonal in 2025 , this time it was the 18-year-old with the symbolic escape. There were three and a half minutes left on Saturday night when it happened. Marc Casadó slipped the ball through, Lamine Yamal ran on to it and Marko Dmitrovic ran out to it. The Barcelona forward blocked the Espanyol goalkeeper’s clearance, the rebounded setting him up and leaving Dmitrovic and everyone else to watch the inevitable. Alone, running free into the area, an open goal before him, Lamine Yamal slowed, smiled, maybe even laughed a little, took in the moment, and raised his arms, Usain Bolt contemplating Richard Thompson and Walter Dix. He had not finished, his team had not either, but he knew. They all did. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: The wait is over: Eta’s arrival as head coach breaks barriers for women in football
source_signals.summary: Marie-Louise Eta has been tasked with saving Union Berlin from Bundesliga relegation in a groundbreaking appointment For Marie-Louise Eta, it may feel like another day at the office. The wider significance will be greater, though, when she oversees Union Berlin in their crunch Bundesliga match against Wolfsburg this weekend. History will be made and another barrier broken: it will be the first time a woman appointed as head coach of a men’s team has taken charge of a fixture in one of Europe’s top five leagues. Eta was given the reins on an interim basis after Steffen Baumgart, her predecessor, was sacked on Saturday. Union had just lost 3-1 to bottom-placed Heidenheim and, with five games left, could not be sure of a late-season dalliance with the drop zone. They are seven points clear of the relegation playoff spot but a run of two wins from 14 games has equated to freefall. Union needed the best person to arrest it, some form of continuity was key, so the 34-year-old Eta, a richly exciting prospect, was an obvious choice. Continue reading...

Noyes’s intervention in the long-running debate over affordability checks comes in an open letter sent to Nandy on Monday. It echoes similar calls for a halt to the process by many senior figures in the racing industry, which fears a disproportionate effect on racing bettors with the potential to cost the industry tens of millions of pounds in income if punters refuse to supply financial information to gambling operators and switch to the black market instead. source_signals.title: History beckons for I Am Maximus as Red Rum’s record comes in to view | Greg Wood
source_signals.summary: The two-time Grand National winner is surely second only to ‘Rummy’ and will be a strong favourite at Aintree in 2027 Relentless drama, no significant injuries to any of the 34 runners and a winner who inked himself on to the exclusive list of Aintree legends with the possibility of better to come next year. Saturday’s Grand National produced everything racing could realistically hope for and more, and if I Am Maximus can stay sound and return to Aintree in 2027 for a fourth run in the race, it promises to be one of the highlights of the sporting year. There was a 45-year wait for the next dual Grand National winner after Red Rum’s second success in 1974. Now, seven years after Tiger Roll’s second victory, I Am Maximus is only the third horse since the mid 1930s to register win number two, and the first since the peerless “Rummy” to win in nonconsecutive years. Having also finished a close second in 2025, he is arguably already second only to Red Rum in the list of all-time Aintree greats. He is a marketing person’s dream: a sporting hero who carries a gladiatorial name into combat, always rises to the challenge in the most famous race of the year, and won’t overturn his car or pick a fight in a nightclub. Rory McIlroy’s second successive Masters win dominates the sporting day, but the wider sports brief stays tightly focused on football transfers and key state of play.

lead_story.title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
lead_story.summary: Péter Magyar has ended Viktor Orbán’s 16-year rule in Hungary, with a landslide that overturns one of Europe’s most durable nationalist governments and could reshape the EU’s response to Moscow, rule-of-law disputes and enlargement politics.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
body: Péter Magyar has ended Viktor Orbán’s 16-year grip on power in Hungary, overturning one of Europe’s most durable nationalist governments and setting up a major political reset in Budapest.

The result is likely to reverberate well beyond Hungary’s borders. Orbán has long been one of the European Union’s most defiant leaders, clashing with Brussels over rule-of-law standards, immigration and support for Ukraine, while keeping a pragmatic line to Moscow.

Magyar, a 45-year-old former insider turned insurgent, persuaded a majority of voters that change was worth the risk. His win suggests a sharp public appetite for a less polarising style of government after years of bitter political division.

For the EU, the shift could alter debates on sanctions, enlargement and funding, especially if Hungary’s next government is more willing to align with its partners. It also raises questions about how quickly Orbán’s political machine can adapt after such a heavy defeat.
The immediate test will be whether Magyar can turn a protest vote into a workable governing majority and whether he can hold together the broad coalition that brought him victory.
If he succeeds, Hungary could move into a new phase in its relations with both Brussels and Moscow; if he fails, Orbán’s influence may still shape the country from opposition.

stories.title: Pope says he has 'no fear' of Trump after scathing criticism
stories.summary: Pope Leo has responded to Donald Trump’s attack by saying he has “no fear”, keeping the Vatican and the White House on a collision course over crime, foreign policy and the Church’s public stance.
stories.source_signal_title: Pope says he has 'no fear' of Trump after scathing criticism
body: Pope Leo has hit back at Donald Trump after the US president accused him of being weak on crime and terrible for foreign policy, saying he has “no fear” in the face of the criticism.

The exchange keeps the Vatican and the White House on a collision course over issues that extend beyond personal sparring. It touches on the Church’s public voice on migration, violence and diplomacy at a time of acute global strain.
Trump’s attack adds to a long history of friction between American political leaders and popes over moral authority, social policy and the language used to discuss public life.
The Vatican has not signalled any retreat from its positions, and Leo’s response suggests he is willing to meet political pressure head-on rather than avoid confrontation.
What happens next will depend on whether the dispute remains a verbal clash or spills into wider disagreements on foreign policy and the Church’s role in public debate.

stories.title: Carney on verge of Liberal majority government as votes cast in three by-elections
stories.summary: Mark Carney is close to securing a Liberal majority in Canada as three by-elections are held, with defections from the Conservatives strengthening his grip on power.
stories.source_signal_title: Carney on verge of Liberal majority government as votes cast in three by-elections
body: Mark Carney is close to securing a Liberal majority in Canada as voters go to the polls in three by-elections, a development that could further strengthen his hold on power.

The stakes are significant because one more seat could help turn his already improved position into something much more durable, especially after several Conservatives crossed the floor and joined the Liberals.

Carney’s advance reflects a broader political reordering in Ottawa, where stability and competence have become powerful selling points. The defections from the Conservatives suggest some lawmakers believe the government is the likelier vehicle for influence.
A majority would give the prime minister more room to govern without relying on fragile deals, but it would also sharpen scrutiny of how he brought together such an unlikely coalition of support.
The result of the by-elections will show whether that momentum is real or merely a temporary advantage.

stories.title: Iran carried out highest number of executions in decades
stories.summary: Iran has recorded its highest number of executions in decades, with rights groups warning that more people could be executed after the anti-government protests earlier this year.
stories.source_signal_title: Iran carried out highest number of executions in decades
body: Iran has carried out its highest number of executions in decades, according to rights groups that say the scale of the killings has risen sharply in the wake of anti-government protests earlier this year.

The figures matter not only because of their severity, but because organisations warn that many more people could still face execution. That has heightened concern about a wider crackdown on dissent as the authorities seek to reassert control.

The executions form part of a long-running pattern in Iran, where the death penalty has often been used as a tool of political intimidation as well as criminal punishment. But the latest surge is being seen as especially alarming by campaigners.
Rights groups are likely to keep pressing for transparency over detainees, charges and appeals, while governments that maintain relations with Tehran may come under renewed pressure to respond.
For families of prisoners and activists inside Iran, the immediate fear is that the worst may not be over.

stories.title: Nigeria Air Force orders investigation after dozens killed in airstrike
stories.summary: Nigeria’s air force has ordered an investigation after an airstrike killed dozens, with reports saying more than 100 people may have died in the attack.
stories.source_signal_title: Nigeria Air Force orders investigation after dozens killed in airstrike
body: Nigeria’s air force has ordered an investigation after an airstrike killed dozens of people, with reports suggesting that more than 100 may have died in the attack.

The episode has revived longstanding fears about civilian casualties in Nigeria’s conflict zones, where military operations against armed groups have repeatedly hit the wrong targets or caused indiscriminate damage.

Authorities have not yet commented publicly on the reported toll, but any confirmed misfire would intensify pressure on the government to explain how the strike was authorised and what safeguards were in place.
The incident is also likely to deepen mistrust in affected communities, especially if local accounts of the death toll prove far higher than official statements.
The inquiry will now be watched closely for whether it is independent, how quickly it reports, and whether anyone is held accountable.

stories.title: Peru election drags into second day after ballot delivery fiasco
stories.summary: Peru’s presidential and congressional election has spilled into a second day after technical and logistical failures disrupted voting, deepening doubts about the country’s electoral administration.
stories.source_signal_title: Peru election drags into second day after ballot delivery fiasco
body: Peru’s presidential and congressional election has spilled into a second day after technical and logistical failures disrupted voting across parts of the country.

The delay has added to concerns about the state of Peru’s electoral administration at a time when trust in institutions is already fragile. Voters were meant to choose both a new president and members of Congress, but some polling stations were unable to operate normally.
The problems are the latest reminder of Peru’s political instability, which has seen repeated changes of government and growing public frustration with official incompetence and corruption.
Election authorities now face pressure to explain what went wrong and to show that delayed voting can still produce a result that is seen as credible.
The outcome may matter as much for confidence in the system as for the winner of the race itself.

stories.title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
stories.summary: At least 30 people are feared dead in a crush at a tourist event in Haiti, where the prime minister said many of the victims were young people.
stories.source_signal_title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
body: At least 30 people are feared dead after a crush at a tourist event in Haiti, where the prime minister said many of the victims were young people.

The incident, described by officials as taking place at an event attended by many young people, is a grim reminder of how quickly large gatherings can turn deadly in a country already grappling with political and security crises.

Haiti has endured years of violence, instability and weak public services, which can make emergency response and crowd control especially difficult. That context makes the scale of the tragedy more acute.
Authorities will now face questions about safety planning, crowd management and whether more could have been done to prevent the crush.
The immediate priority is search, treatment and identification, but the wider test will be whether the state can provide answers as well as condolences.

stories.title: Oil jumps back above $100 after US-Iran peace talks fail
stories.summary: Oil prices have risen back above $100 a barrel after the collapse of US-Iran talks, intensifying the risk of a broader energy shock for markets, shipping and consumers.
stories.source_signal_title: Oil jumps back above $100 after US-Iran peace talks fail
body: Oil prices have climbed back above $100 a barrel after US-Iran talks collapsed at the weekend, raising the prospect of a broader energy shock for markets and consumers.

The failure of the negotiations has revived fears that supply routes and tanker traffic could be disrupted further if tensions keep rising in the Gulf. That risk matters well beyond energy traders, because higher crude prices can feed through to transport, food and inflation.

For shipping firms, airlines and refiners, the immediate concern is whether the Strait of Hormuz remains open enough to keep crude moving. For households, the danger is renewed pressure on petrol, heating and food bills.

The move above $100 underlines how quickly geopolitical shocks can spill into the wider economy. The next question is whether the price spike proves temporary or becomes the start of a prolonged squeeze.

stories.title: How the Iran war affects your money and bills
stories.summary: The Iran war is pushing up petrol, household energy and food costs, putting fresh pressure on household budgets in the UK and beyond.
stories.source_signal_title: How the Iran war affects your money and bills
body: The Iran war is already feeding into everyday costs, pushing up petrol prices, household energy bills and some food prices as markets react to the risk of a wider disruption in supply.

For consumers in the UK and elsewhere, the impact tends to arrive through familiar channels: higher fuel at the pump, dearer shipping and, eventually, more expensive goods in shops. The effect is often uneven, but it can be felt quickly by households with tight budgets.

Energy markets are especially sensitive because the conflict threatens one of the world’s key transport routes. That leaves governments, traders and suppliers trying to judge whether current price rises are a short-lived reaction or the start of something more persistent.
The key uncertainty is how long the conflict lasts and whether it spreads further. If tensions remain elevated, the pressure on bills could linger well beyond the immediate headlines.

stories.title: US home buyers 'frozen' as sales slump over Iran war fears
stories.summary: US home sales have fallen to a nine-month low as Iran war fears add to uncertainty, leaving buyers cautious and economists warning of a deeper slowdown.
stories.source_signal_title: US home buyers 'frozen' as sales slump over Iran war fears
body: US home sales have fallen to a nine-month low as fears over the Iran war add to already fragile buyer confidence.

The drop suggests that uncertainty is not just affecting energy and shipping markets, but also the decisions of ordinary households. When buyers hesitate, the housing market can slow quickly, and that in turn can spill into construction, lending and local economies.

Economists are warning that the slowdown could deepen if geopolitical anxiety continues to weigh on sentiment. Higher borrowing costs and broader economic uncertainty are already making home purchases harder for many families.
The slump matters because housing is often a leading indicator of consumer confidence. If buyers stay frozen, the drag could spread beyond property into the wider US economy.

stories.title: EasyJet passengers describe EU border 'nightmare'
stories.summary: EasyJet passengers have faced long queues and missed flights as the new EU digital border control system caused disruption at Milan’s Linate airport and raised wider airline concerns.
stories.source_signal_title: EasyJet passengers describe EU border 'nightmare'
body: EasyJet passengers have described a border-control “nightmare” at Milan’s Linate airport after the new EU digital border system caused long queues and missed flights.

The disruption highlights the practical problems that can come with new border technology, even when the policy aims are administrative rather than political. Airlines say they fear further problems as the system is rolled out more widely.
For passengers, the impact is immediate: delays, confusion and the risk of being left behind. EasyJet has already faced complaints from people who missed flights during the queues.

The wider concern is whether airports and airlines are ready for the scale of change. If the early teething problems persist, pressure will grow on Brussels and national authorities to slow implementation or improve staffing and systems.

stories.title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: As Donald Trump threatens a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, this explainer sets out what a naval blockade means in practice, and why it would matter for oil, shipping and war risk.
stories.source_signal_title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
body: Donald Trump says the US is blockading the Strait of Hormuz, a claim that has put renewed focus on what a naval blockade actually means in practice.

In simple terms, a blockade is an attempt to stop ships entering or leaving an area by force or threat of force. In the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil chokepoints, that would have immediate consequences for global shipping and energy prices.

The practical questions are just as important as the legal ones: who enforces the blockade, how tightly it is policed, and how other states or shipping companies respond. Even the prospect of a blockade can push up insurance costs and unsettle markets.
The issue matters because the Strait carries huge volumes of oil and fuel. If access is restricted for long, the economic effect could spread far beyond the Gulf.
What happens next depends on whether the threat remains rhetoric or becomes an operational reality.

stories.title: Markets & Economy update 6
stories.summary: A significant development in markets & economy with wider consequences under review.
stories.source_signal_title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
body: The biggest market story in view remains the fallout from the crisis around Iran, with oil prices, shipping costs and airline shares all moving sharply as investors reassess risk.

That matters because energy shocks do not stay confined to commodity markets. They quickly feed into inflation expectations, consumer bills and business costs, which is why governments and central banks are watching developments so closely.

The wider point is that geopolitics is now driving market pricing as much as economic data. Traders are trying to judge whether this is a short, violent correction or the start of a longer period of disruption.
For businesses and households, the next few days will be shaped by the same question: how far can the shock spread before policy makers or diplomacy bring some relief?

stories.title: Green light to build nuclear power station that promises 8,000 jobs
stories.summary: Rolls-Royce has won approval to build a nuclear power station that it says could create 8,000 jobs across Britain, giving a major boost to the UK’s energy and industrial strategy.
stories.source_signal_title: Green light to build nuclear power station that promises 8,000 jobs
body: Rolls-Royce has won approval to build a nuclear power station that it says could create 8,000 jobs across Britain, giving a major boost to the UK’s energy plans and industrial strategy.

The project is significant because ministers see new nuclear capacity as part of a broader answer to energy security, decarbonisation and high-skilled employment. Supporters say it could help rebuild domestic supply chains as well as power generation.

The promise of 8,000 jobs will be politically attractive, but such projects are also known for long timelines, cost pressures and delivery risk. What is approved on paper can still face delays in construction and financing.
The next phase will be about planning, local support and whether the project can move from announcement to actual building work.
For the UK, this is as much a test of execution as ambition.

stories.title: Rolls-Royce secures nearly £600m in UK government cash to develop small reactors
stories.summary: Rolls-Royce has secured up to £599m from Britain’s national wealth fund to develop small modular reactors at Wylfa in Wales, a key test for the UK’s nuclear ambitions.
stories.source_signal_title: Rolls-Royce secures nearly £600m in UK government cash to develop small reactors
body: Rolls-Royce has secured up to £599m from Britain’s national wealth fund to develop small modular reactors at Wylfa in Wales, marking a major step in the UK’s nuclear ambitions.
The money will support the company’s work on small reactor design at the Anglesey site, which has become a key reference point for Britain’s efforts to expand low-carbon power and industrial jobs.

The investment is important because small modular reactors are still an emerging technology, and the UK wants to be among the first countries to commercialise them. That makes Wylfa a test case for whether the sector can move beyond policy rhetoric.
For the government, the deal offers a chance to show it can back both energy security and regional development. For Rolls-Royce, it is a critical milestone but not yet proof of delivery.
The real challenge now is turning finance into a working plant.

stories.title: Booking.com warns customers of hack that exposed their data
stories.summary: Booking.com says a cyber breach exposed some customers’ booking information, raising fresh questions about online travel security and data protection.
stories.source_signal_title: Booking.com warns customers of hack that exposed their data
body: Booking.com has warned customers that a cyber breach exposed some booking information, raising fresh concerns about the security of online travel platforms.

The company says unauthorised parties gained access to some guests’ details, including booking information. While the full scale has not been made public, even limited exposure can leave customers vulnerable to scams and phishing attempts.

The incident adds to a run of data breaches across major consumer services, where attackers increasingly target large databases of personal and contact information. That makes trust and data protection central commercial issues, not just technical ones.
For travellers, the immediate advice is likely to be vigilance over emails, messages and payment accounts. For the company, the bigger challenge is restoring confidence after another breach.
Questions will now focus on how the access happened and whether customers were notified quickly enough.

stories.title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
stories.summary: The UK government is preparing legislation that could let ministers adopt evolving EU single market rules, raising questions about parliamentary scrutiny and the post-Brexit settlement.
stories.source_signal_title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
body: The UK government is preparing legislation that could allow ministers to adopt evolving EU single market rules, a notable shift in the post-Brexit settlement.

The move matters because it could make it easier to deliver planned agreements with the EU, but it also raises questions about how much Parliament would be able to scrutinise future rule changes. That tension goes to the heart of the Brexit debate over sovereignty and flexibility.
Supporters will argue that closer alignment can reduce friction for business and smooth trade. Critics are likely to say the approach risks importing rules without proper democratic oversight.

The politics are delicate because any move towards dynamic alignment will be judged against the promises made during the Brexit process. The details of how the legislation works will therefore matter as much as the headline.
What happens next will depend on whether ministers can persuade MPs that the benefits outweigh the constitutional concern.

stories.title: Southport killer's parents failed in 'moral duty' to report son
stories.summary: A Southport inquiry has found that the killer’s parents failed in their “moral duty” to report him, with the report citing catastrophic consequences and systemic failures.
stories.source_signal_title: Southport killer's parents failed in 'moral duty' to report son
body: A Southport inquiry has found that the killer’s parents failed in their “moral duty” to report him, concluding that their failure to recognise the danger he posed had catastrophic consequences.

The findings are a stark reminder that the background to violent attacks often includes warning signs that are missed, minimised or not acted upon in time. The report also points to broader systemic failures alongside family responsibility.

For the families of the victims, the inquiry will be about answers as well as accountability. For the wider public, it raises difficult questions about how concerns about risk should be handled when they arise at home, in schools or in community settings.
The report is likely to intensify debate over safeguarding, intervention and the limits of what families can realistically do when they fear a relative may be dangerous.
The next stage will be whether authorities act on the lessons quickly enough to prevent future failures.

stories.title: PM to scrap spy chiefs' Hillsborough Law veto
stories.summary: Keir Starmer plans to remove the veto that allowed security chiefs to block spies from giving evidence to future public inquiries, a significant change after long-running campaign pressure.
stories.source_signal_title: PM to scrap spy chiefs' Hillsborough Law veto
body: Keir Starmer plans to remove the veto that allowed security chiefs to block spies from giving evidence to future public inquiries, a significant change to the way sensitive cases could be examined.

The move is important because it goes to the balance between national security and public accountability. Campaigners who have pushed for the change will see it as a step towards making sure inquiries can get to the truth more fully.

The change is also part of the long shadow of Hillsborough, where mistrust of institutions and demands for candour have shaped reform efforts for years. Removing the veto would limit the ability of security agencies to shield witnesses from scrutiny.
The government will still have to show how it protects genuinely sensitive material while ensuring witnesses can be heard.
If the reform goes ahead, it could alter how the state responds to future disasters and allegations of misconduct.

stories.title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
stories.summary: England’s school dinner rules are being tightened, with deep-fried food banned and more vegetables and whole grains required as ministers try to curb childhood obesity.
stories.source_signal_title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
body: England’s school dinner rules are being tightened, with deep-fried food to be banned and schools told to provide more vegetables, whole grains and less sugar.
The changes are aimed at tackling childhood obesity and improving diet quality in classrooms. They form part of a wider effort to make school meals healthier and more consistent across the country.

What children eat at school matters because it can shape habits, nutrition and concentration, especially for pupils who rely heavily on school meals. The policy also signals a stronger state role in setting nutritional standards.
Some schools may welcome the clearer guidance, while others will worry about cost, kitchen capacity and whether pupils will accept the changes.
The question now is whether the reforms improve health in practice, not just on paper.

stories.title: Fried nuggets and steamed sponges off menu in school food overhaul in England
stories.summary: The first major update to England’s school food standards in 13 years will ban items such as fried nuggets and steamed sponges from September, in a push to improve children’s diets.
stories.source_signal_title: Fried nuggets and steamed sponges off menu in school food overhaul in England
body: England is preparing the first major update to school food standards in 13 years, with items such as fried nuggets and steamed sponges set to be removed from menus from September.

The overhaul is part of a broader push to improve children’s diets and help lower obesity rates. Campaigners have welcomed the changes, arguing that school food policy can make a real difference to long-term health.
The timing matters because childhood obesity remains a major public health issue, with pressure growing on ministers to show they can act on prevention rather than simply treatment later on.

For schools, the immediate challenge will be cost, compliance and keeping meals appealing as well as healthier. For parents, the reforms may be seen as overdue or overly prescriptive, depending on their view of state intervention.
The deeper question is whether better standards can translate into better habits outside the school gate.

stories.title: Roblox defends expanded age‑checks after parents raise concerns over errors
stories.summary: Roblox says its expanded age-check system is aimed at protecting its 144 million daily users, even as parents warn that automated errors can wrongly classify accounts.
stories.source_signal_title: Roblox defends expanded age‑checks after parents raise concerns over errors
body: Roblox has defended its expanded age-check system, saying it is intended to protect its 144 million daily users even as parents warn that automated errors can wrongly classify accounts.

The company is extending its technology to create age-specific accounts, part of a wider effort to limit contact between children and adults online. That reflects growing pressure on platforms to do more about child safety.
But the controversy shows the difficulty of using automated systems at scale. If age checks misfire, legitimate users can be pushed into the wrong category, creating frustration and mistrust.

For parents, the concern is whether the safeguards are accurate enough to be worth the disruption. For Roblox, the issue is whether it can prove that stronger protection does not come at the cost of fairness and usability.
The debate is likely to continue as regulators and families demand better online safeguards.

stories.title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
stories.summary: Meta has removed Facebook ads recruiting plaintiffs for social-media addiction lawsuits after losing a landmark California case, highlighting growing legal pressure on big platforms.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
body: Meta has removed Facebook ads that were recruiting plaintiffs for social-media addiction lawsuits, increasing the pressure on the company after a landmark defeat in California.

The decision matters because it comes as social media firms face growing legal scrutiny over the way their products are designed and used, especially by younger users. The ads appeared to be part of wider litigation efforts against the platform.
Meta has already been forced to confront claims that its services can be addictive or harmful, and the California case may encourage more lawsuits or settlement pressure.

For the company, taking the ads down may reduce immediate controversy, but it does not remove the underlying legal risk. For claimants and campaigners, the fight is moving from publicity to the courts.
The next step will be whether similar cases gain traction elsewhere.

stories.title: OpenAI boss Sam Altman's home targeted with Molotov cocktail
stories.summary: San Francisco police have arrested a 20-year-old suspect after OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman’s home was targeted with a Molotov cocktail attack.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI boss Sam Altman's home targeted with Molotov cocktail
body: San Francisco police have arrested a 20-year-old suspect after OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman’s home was targeted with a Molotov cocktail attack.

The incident is serious not only because it involved a senior figure in the artificial intelligence industry, but because it reflects the increasingly volatile public atmosphere around powerful technology companies and their leaders.

Police said a perimeter gate was set alight, and the swift arrest suggests they believe the case can be investigated as a specific criminal act rather than a random threat. The motive has not been made public.
The attack is likely to prompt renewed security concerns around high-profile tech executives, especially in the San Francisco Bay Area, where resentment and fascination can sit uneasily together.
What remains unclear is whether the assault was personal, political or linked to broader anger at AI firms.

stories.title: Amazon to end support for older Kindles, prompting user outcry
stories.summary: Amazon will end support for Kindles released before 2013, leaving some owners unable to download new e-books and prompting customer anger.
stories.source_signal_title: Amazon to end support for older Kindles, prompting user outcry
body: Amazon will end support for Kindles released before 2013, leaving some owners unable to download new e-books and prompting a wave of user anger.

The move matters because it highlights the life cycle of digital devices: hardware may still function, but access to services can be withdrawn. That can turn a working product into a limited one almost overnight.

For readers, the immediate effect is practical rather than symbolic. Older devices may still display existing libraries, but the loss of new downloads will inconvenience customers who have relied on them for years.
The decision also adds to a wider debate about planned obsolescence, software support and how much control companies should retain over products already sold.
Amazon may argue that support decisions are routine, but the outcry shows how sensitive customers are when a familiar device is effectively left behind.

stories.title: McIlroy holds nerve to win second successive Masters
stories.summary: Rory McIlroy has won a second successive Masters at Augusta, beating Scottie Scheffler by one shot and underlining his status as one of golf’s modern greats.
stories.source_signal_title: McIlroy holds nerve to win second successive Masters
body: Rory McIlroy has won a second successive Masters at Augusta, beating Scottie Scheffler by one shot after a tense final round.

The victory underlines McIlroy’s status as one of golf’s modern greats and adds another major title to a career that has already placed him among the game’s elite. Winning back-to-back Masters is rare, and it strengthens the sense that he is still adding to his legacy rather than merely preserving it.

The final round brought enough pressure to test him, but he held his nerve when it mattered most. For Scheffler, the defeat will sting, but the quality of the contest underlined how high the bar now is at the top of the men’s game.
The result will fuel fresh debate about where McIlroy stands historically and whether more majors are now in reach.
For Augusta, it was another reminder that the biggest prizes can still hinge on a single shot.

stories.title: Where does McIlroy rank among greats after Masters defence?
stories.summary: After defending the Masters title, Rory McIlroy now has six major wins and joins a tiny group of repeat champions, prompting fresh debate about his place in golf history.
stories.source_signal_title: Where does McIlroy rank among greats after Masters defence?
body: After defending the Masters title, Rory McIlroy now has six major wins and belongs to a very small group of repeat champions, which has revived debate about where he stands in golf history.
The comparison is not just statistical. Back-to-back Masters wins are rare, and combining that with a career Grand Slam places McIlroy in especially select company among the sport’s modern figures.

The question now is whether this is a defining peak or a stage on the way to even greater numbers. History in golf is often measured by major totals, consistency and longevity, and McIlroy has moved deeper into that conversation.
The article of record around him is likely to keep shifting as he continues to play at the top level. Every added title changes the argument.
For now, the defence of the Masters confirms that his place among the greats is no longer theoretical.

stories.title: Tottenham in advanced talks to sign Robertson
stories.summary: Tottenham are in advanced talks over a free transfer for Andy Robertson, a notable Premier League move with Liverpool’s defender potentially available this summer.
stories.source_signal_title: Tottenham in advanced talks to sign Robertson
body: Tottenham are in advanced talks to sign Andy Robertson on a free transfer, a move that would be one of the more notable Premier League deals of the summer.

Robertson’s possible availability has given Spurs a chance to strengthen a key area without paying a fee, but the transfer would still be a significant statement about their ambitions and Liverpool’s squad transition.

For a player of Robertson’s experience, any move would carry immediate sporting weight as well as symbolic value. He has been a central figure in Liverpool’s recent success, so his departure would mark a shift in the balance of the side.
The main uncertainty is whether talks progress to an agreement and how much competition Tottenham face if the deal opens up.
If it happens, it would be one of the headline defensive moves of the window.

stories.title: Spurs captain Romero out for rest of season with injury
stories.summary: Tottenham captain Cristian Romero is set to miss the rest of the season after the knee injury he suffered in the defeat at Sunderland.
stories.source_signal_title: Spurs captain Romero out for rest of season with injury
body: Tottenham captain Cristian Romero is set to miss the rest of the season after the knee injury he suffered in the defeat at Sunderland.

The loss is a major blow because Romero is not only a first-choice defender but also a leadership figure at Spurs. An extended absence will affect the team’s structure, resilience and likely plans for the run-in.
Injuries to key players can reshape a season very quickly, especially when they involve the captain and a central defensive pillar. Tottenham now have to adjust both tactically and emotionally.
The club will hope the recovery timeline does not stretch beyond the current prognosis, but knee injuries can be unpredictable.
For Spurs, the immediate task is to stabilise results without one of their most important players.
